footer.copy: An open community network of air-quality sensors with public map views and device measurement history.

home.lead: Air360 collects data from sensors installed by project contributors at home, at work, and around the city. Build your own sensor, connect it, and it will appear on the shared map.

build.title: Device assembly
build.assemblyBody: You can assemble the device with the Air360 shield board or wire sensors directly to the ESP32-S3. The shield reduces wiring mistakes; direct wiring is useful for prototypes, debugging, and custom enclosures.

options.label: Option 1
options.title: Build with the shield board
options.body: The shield breaks power and signal lines out to labeled connectors. This is the primary user path for repeatable builds.
notes: Order the board from Gerber files or from the OSHWLab project.
Check the board revision against this guide before soldering.
After assembly, sensors connect to the labeled shield connectors.

options.label: Option 2
options.title: Wire directly to ESP32-S3
options.body: Connect sensors to GPIOs directly using the table below. This path requires careful power, ground, and shared-bus wiring.
notes: For multiple I2C devices, use a branched I2C bus or a dedicated hub.
Search for "I2C hub" on AliExpress, Amazon, or similar marketplaces.
All signal lines must be compatible with ESP32-S3 3.3 V logic.
UART1 is shared by GPS and the cellular modem by default, so use separate UART ports if both are installed.

build.shieldTitle: Option 1: build with the shield board
build.shieldBody: The shield board sits on top of the ESP32-S3 and breaks power, I2C, UART, and auxiliary lines out to connectors. These are the baseline parts needed to assemble the board.

shieldParts.component: DS1023-1x22S21, 1x22 female socket header, 2.54 mm pitch
shieldParts.use: Headers for mounting the shield board onto the ESP32-S3.

shieldParts.component: Single row male 2.54 mm breakable pin header
shieldParts.use: Male pin headers for auxiliary pins and jumpers.
shieldParts.search: 2.54mm single row breakable male pin header

shieldParts.component: DC-005, 5.5x2.1 mm PCB-mount DC barrel jack
shieldParts.use: DC power input on the shield board.
shieldParts.search: DC-005 5.5x2.1mm PCB mount DC power jack

shieldParts.use: Optional cable connectors for SPS30 and SCD30.
shieldParts.search: JST ZH 1.5mm 4 pin 5 pin connector housing crimp terminal

shieldParts.use: Screw terminal for the INA219 power measurement path.

shieldParts.quantity: 1 pc if INA219 is not used
shieldParts.use: Jumper in place of the INA219 path when current sensing is not installed.

build.directBody: The table below follows the current firmware defaults. Some UART and GPIO sensors can be reassigned in the device web UI, but these values are the best starting point for a first build.
directNotes: I2C: all I2C sensors share SDA=GPIO8 and SCL=GPIO9.
GPIO and analog sensors use one of GPIO4, GPIO5, or GPIO6.

wiringRows.note: Use one shared I2C bus for multiple modules; sensor addresses must not conflict.

wiringRows.pins: RX=GPIO16, TX=GPIO15; UART1 RX=GPIO18, TX=GPIO17 is selectable
wiringRows.note: Sensor TX goes to ESP32-S3 RX; sensor RX goes to ESP32-S3 TX.

wiringRows.pins: RX=GPIO18, TX=GPIO17; UART2 RX=GPIO16, TX=GPIO15 is selectable
wiringRows.note: UART1 conflicts with the SIM7600E default pins.
Select one available pin in the sensor settings.

wiringRows.pins: P1/yellow signal to GPIO4 by default; GPIO5 or GPIO6 selectable

wiringRows.note: RESET is not wired by default; do not use GPS on UART1 at the same time.

build.sensorGuideBody: Pick one or more sensors based on what you want to monitor. Sensors can be combined freely; values go to the Air360 API, and compatible values also go to Sensor.Community.

sensors.name: BME280
sensors.why: Compact all-in-one climate sensor; good for indoor and outdoor baseline monitoring.

sensors.name: BME680
sensors.why: Same as BME280 with a gas sensor that estimates indoor air quality and volatile organic compounds.

sensors.name: AHT30
sensors.why: Accurate and low-cost; a reliable upgrade over DHT22 with I2C connectivity.

sensors.name: SHT3X
sensors.why: High-accuracy sensor; more consistent than AHT30 across wide temperature ranges.

sensors.name: SHT4X
sensors.why: Improved successor to SHT3X with higher accuracy in humid environments.

sensors.name: HTU2X
sensors.why: Budget option; lower accuracy than SHT series but widely available.

sensors.name: DHT11
sensors.why: Entry-level sensor with limited range (0–50 °C, 20–90 %RH); suitable for learning or quick tests.

sensors.name: DHT22
sensors.why: Better range and accuracy than DHT11; common first sensor for prototypes.

sensors.name: DS18B20
sensors.why: Waterproof probe; designed for outdoor, soil, or liquid temperature measurement.

sensors.name: SPS30
sensors.measures: PM1.0, PM2.5, PM4.0, PM10, particle count, typical particle size
sensors.why: Most complete PM sensor in the lineup; recommended for outdoor air quality stations.

sensors.name: SDS011
sensors.why: Affordable laser sensor; popular for city and roadside air monitoring.

sensors.name: PMSX003 (PMS5003 / PMS7003)
sensors.measures: PM1.0, PM2.5, PM10, particle count bins
sensors.why: Widely available compact sensor; good balance of cost and data completeness.

sensors.name: PPD42NS
sensors.why: Basic optical sensor; lower accuracy; GPIO-based, no shared bus required.

sensors.name: SCD30
sensors.why: NDIR CO₂ sensor; accurate and stable; recommended for ventilation and indoor air quality monitoring.

sensors.name: MH-Z19B
sensors.why: NDIR CO₂ via UART; compact and common for home automation projects.

sensors.name: VEML7700
sensors.why: Measures ambient light level; useful for daylight tracking or controlling light-dependent devices.

sensors.name: OPT3001
sensors.why: Alternative to VEML7700 with a slightly different spectral response; same use cases.

sensors.name: INA219
sensors.why: Monitors the device's own power consumption; useful for solar-powered or battery-backed setups.

build.sensorGuideGpsBody: A GPS module (any NMEA-compatible receiver via UART) adds geographic coordinates to every measurement batch. Use it for fixed outdoor stations where you want precise coordinates attached to each reading, or for mobile setups mounted on a vehicle or carried as a portable unit.
build.sensorGuideCellularBody: A cellular modem lets the device upload data over a SIM card with no Wi-Fi required. Supported models: SIM7600 (default), SIM7070, SIM7000, BG96, EC20, SIM800, and Generic (any AT-command modem via esp-modem). This is the right choice for rooftop stations, remote field installations, or any location where a stable Wi-Fi connection is not available.
build.enclosureBody: For outdoor installation, you can 3D-print a Stevenson-screen-style enclosure. It shields the device from direct sun and rain while keeping airflow around the sensors.
build.enclosureImageAlt: Air360 in a Stevenson screen enclosure
build.powerBody: Air360 runs on 5 V. Choose whichever supply suits your installation.

powerOptions.label: Option 1
powerOptions.title: Mains power (5 V adapter)
powerOptions.body: Connect any 5 V USB power adapter. Two connection points are available depending on your build.
notes: Via the USB-C port on the ESP32-S3 — works for both direct-wiring and shield builds.
Via the DC-005 barrel jack (5.5 × 2.1 mm) on the shield board.

powerOptions.label: Option 2
powerOptions.title: Solar panel with MPPT charger
powerOptions.body: For off-grid and outdoor deployments. The setup uses a custom MPPT module based on the CN3722 that charges a battery from a solar panel and supplies regulated 5 V to the device via power-path switching — the device stays on at night and on overcast days.
notes: 3D-printable solar panel mount and MPPT module enclosure are on Printables — assembly instructions and a link to the PCB are on the same page.
Read the full CN3722 article before sourcing components — values depend on your battery chemistry and cell count.

build.compatibilityBody: Air360 firmware keeps backward compatibility with Sensor.Community: the device can still upload to the legacy Sensor.Community endpoint using the same short device ID registered on devices.sensor.community. Air360 API is broader and accepts every current sensor type.

compatibilityRows.sensorCommunity: Yes: temperature, humidity, pressure; gas resistance is skipped
compatibilityRows.air360Api: Yes: all sensor values in a typed JSON batch

compatibilityRows.sensorCommunity: Yes: PM1.0, PM2.5, PM4.0, PM10, number concentration bins, typical particle size
Partial: PM1.0, PM2.5, PM10; particle-count bins are skipped
Partial: latitude, longitude, altitude; satellites/speed/course/HDOP are skipped

compatibilityRows.sensor: AHT30, VEML7700, OPT3001, PPD42NS, INA219, MH-Z19B
compatibilityRows.sensorCommunity: No: the Sensor.Community adapter skips these types
compatibilityRows.air360Api: Yes: all values pass through without sensor-type filtering

build.differencesBody: The firmware keeps the Sensor.Community flow working, but adds capabilities that do not fit the older upload format.

differenceItems.title: Sensor.Community stays available
differenceItems.body: You can still register the device on devices.sensor.community and upload compatible measurements to their API.

differenceItems.title: Air360 API accepts every sensor
differenceItems.body: The native backend receives one typed JSON batch with sensor_type, kind, and timestamp, including values that have no legacy Sensor.Community mapping.

differenceItems.title: Cellular modem out of the box
differenceItems.body: SIM7600 and other esp-modem types are managed by a dedicated cellular runtime: PPP, reconnect backoff, PWRKEY/SLEEP GPIO, and diagnostics in the web UI.

differenceItems.title: Power and extended sensors
differenceItems.body: INA219, VEML7700/OPT3001, PPD42NS, MH-Z19B, and other sensors are available in firmware and Air360 API even when Sensor.Community does not accept them.

differenceItems.title: Local web UI
differenceItems.body: Wi-Fi, cellular, sensor configuration, upload backends, OTA, and diagnostics are available through the device web interface.

differenceItems.title: Multiple backend uploads
differenceItems.body: In addition to Sensor.Community, the firmware supports Air360 API, Custom Upload, and InfluxDB, each with separate configuration and delivery status.

build.firmwareBody: The easiest way to start is to flash the ready-made merged full.bin image with the browser-based ESP Flash tool. After first boot, the device starts a Wi-Fi setup access point.
build.firmwarePanelTitle: Flash, connect to setup AP, then open the device in a browser

firmwareAccess.label: Setup Wi-Fi
firmwareAccess.note: Access point name after a clean flash.

firmwareAccess.label: Device URL
firmwareAccess.note: Open the device web interface by hostname or by IP address.

firmwareSteps.title: Download full.bin
firmwareSteps.body: Open Air360 releases and download the current full.bin. This is the complete merged image for first-time flashing.

firmwareSteps.title: Flash via USB
firmwareSteps.body: Open espflash.app, connect the ESP32-S3 by USB, select the serial port and the full.bin file, then start flashing. Wait for the process to finish.

firmwareSteps.title: First boot — setup AP
firmwareSteps.body: The device reboots. The LED turns pink — it is in setup AP mode. Connect your phone or laptop to the air360 Wi-Fi network (password: air360password).

firmwareSteps.title: Configure Wi-Fi
firmwareSteps.body: A setup page opens automatically after connecting to the AP. Select your Wi-Fi network from the list, enter the password, and tap Save.

firmwareSteps.title: Verify connection
firmwareSteps.body: The device reboots and joins your network. Green LED = connected. Pink LED = connection failed — check the password and try again.

firmwareSteps.title: Open the device
firmwareSteps.body: Go to http://air360.local or http://192.168.4.1 in your browser to configure sensors and upload backends.

firmwareLedColors.meaning: Setup AP mode — no Wi-Fi configured, or connection to Wi-Fi failed.
Fatal boot error — check serial logs.

build.uiGuideBody: After the device joins your Wi-Fi network, open http://air360.local or the device IP in a browser. The interface has four sections.

uiSections.title: Overview
uiSections.body: A read-only dashboard showing device health, network mode, IP address, uptime, and current time. Sensor readings and backend upload results — last status and upload time — are listed here. This is the first page to check when something does not look right.
uiSections.imageAlt: Overview page in the Air360 firmware web interface

uiSections.title: Device
uiSections.body: Network and device settings: Wi-Fi credentials, device name, static IP, cellular modem (APN, modem type, credentials), BLE advertising, and Wi-Fi power save. The firmware update card at the bottom of this page lets you upload a new .bin file directly from the browser without USB access.
uiSections.imageAlt: Device page in the Air360 firmware web interface

uiSections.title: Sensors
uiSections.body: Add, configure, and remove sensors. For each sensor you choose the model, poll interval, and connection (I2C address, UART port, or GPIO pin). Current state and latest readings are shown on the sensor card. In most cases changes apply immediately without a reboot.
uiSections.imageAlt: Sensors page in the Air360 firmware web interface

uiSections.title: Backends
uiSections.body: Configure where measurements are uploaded: Sensor.Community, Air360 API, Custom Upload, or InfluxDB. Each backend can be enabled or disabled independently. Set the upload interval and see the last upload result per backend.
notes: Sensor.Community: the device's Sensor ID is shown on this page. Register it at devices.sensor.community/sensors to link the device to your Sensor.Community account and start seeing measurements on the map.

Air360 API: air360.ru does not require registration. The device authenticates with an Upload secret — generate it on first setup and save it somewhere safe. If you reflash the device, reuse the same secret so your measurement history stays linked to the device.

Air360 API: device coordinates are required before uploads begin. Use the map picker or enter latitude and longitude manually. Adding altitude above sea level is optional but recommended — it improves atmospheric pressure accuracy for stations at elevation.
uiSections.imageAlt: Backends page in the Air360 firmware web interface

build.firmwareDocsBody: For web UI setup, sensors, backend uploads, diagnostics, and OTA, use the full firmware user guide.

mapPage.empty: No active devices with valid coordinates were returned by the backend.

legend.countEstimate: PPD42NS shows the current dust level.

devicePopup.stale: Device has not connected for more than 1 hour.

deviceDetail.chartCount: {count, plural, =0 {0 charts} =1 {1 chart} other {# charts}} for selected period
deviceDetail.noChartBody: The backend did not return measurements for this device and period.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.lead: Air360 is a public portal for environmental monitoring. This policy describes how we handle device data, visitor logs, and third-party map tile requests.
privacy.introductionBody: Air360 is a public portal for environmental monitoring. This Privacy Policy describes how we handle data collected through the portal.
privacy.deviceDataBody: Air360 is a network of IoT devices that collect environmental measurements in real time. Each device may report:
privacy.deviceDataPublic: All of this data is public and displayed on the portal map. No personal information is associated with these measurements.
privacy.visitorDataBody: When you visit the portal, our server automatically records standard access log information:
privacy.visitorDataRetention: These logs are not linked to your identity, are retained for up to 90 days, and are used only for technical diagnostics and security.
privacy.cookiesBody: Air360 does not use tracking or advertising cookies. The portal may set technically necessary cookies required for basic functionality.
privacy.mapTilesBody: The map is rendered with MapLibre GL, which may request map tiles from a third-party tile server. Those requests may include your IP address and the map coordinates you are viewing.
privacy.sharingBody: Air360 does not sell, rent, or share visitor data with third parties for commercial purposes. Data may be disclosed only when required by applicable law, such as in response to a lawful request from law enforcement.
privacy.securityBody: Air360 applies standard technical measures to protect its systems and data. The portal uses HTTPS to encrypt data in transit between your browser and our server.
privacy.contactBody: If you have questions about this Privacy Policy or how we handle data, please contact us at:
visitorDataItems: Date and time of the request
